9	# Feature: Task Execution
10	
11	**ID:** FEAT-010
12	**Status:** In Progress
13	**Priority:** P1
14	**Owner:** DDx Team
15	
16	## Overview
17	
18	DDx owns the task execution lifecycle. The lifecycle is made of concrete layers
19	with stable public names: `run`, `try`, and `work`. Each higher layer composes
20	the layer beneath it. There are no other run kinds beyond those named layers.
21	
22	| Layer | CLI | Inputs | Outputs / side effects | Owns |
23	|---|---|---|---|---|
24	| 1 | `ddx run` | prompt + execution config | structured output, side effects, run metadata (tokens, model, duration, exit) | invocation atom; consumes upstream Fizeau execution contract |
25	| 2 | `ddx try <bead>` | bead id, base revision | new worktree state + run evidence; merge or preserve | worktree start/end capture; bead → prompt resolution; side-effect bundling |
26	| 3 | `ddx work` | bead queue, stop conditions | sequence of `ddx try` records + loop-level record (drained / blocked / deferred) | mechanical queue drain; no-progress detection |
27	
28	`ddx try` wraps `ddx run`. `ddx work` iterates `ddx try`. One on-disk
29	substrate; layer metadata distinguishes records.
30	
31	Implementation code for this boundary should follow the task-execution
32	vocabulary (`taskexec` for core layer logic, `fizeauadapter` for the service
33	bridge). Treat `internal/agent` as legacy naming only; new DDx-owned execution
34	work should make the workflow boundary explicit.
35	
36	`ddx artifact regenerate <id>` is sugar over layer 1 (or layer 2 when the
37	generator edits the repo) with `produces_artifact: <id>` metadata. It is
38	not a fourth layer — it is a labeled invocation at an existing layer.
39	
40	## Agent Interaction Modes
41	
42	DDx defines four named agent interaction modes (`DDX_MODE`) to separate
43	broad interactive queue stewardship from worker execution. The mode governs
44	what an agent opened in a DDx project is permitted to do.
45	
46	| DDX_MODE | Description | Allowed actions | Prohibited actions |
47	|---|---|---|---|
48	| `queue_steward` | Default interactive mode. Surveys, triages, and advises on the queue without claiming or executing beads. | Read tracker and docs; report status; advise on bead quality; suggest readiness fixes; run `ddx bead list/ready/status/show`. | Claiming beads; creating isolated workspaces; starting `ddx work` or `ddx try` without an explicit user directive. |
49	| `bead_execution` | Worker mode. Executes one or more beads from the queue in isolated workspaces. | Full FEAT-010 layer-2 / layer-3 lifecycle: workspace creation, bead attempt, merge or preserve, evidence bundle. | Scope creep outside the named bead; switching to direct implementation without a bead. |
50	| `direct_user_implementation` | Human-directed implementation. The user has explicitly asked the agent to edit code or docs, bypassing the bead queue. | Edit code and docs as instructed; commit to the current branch. | Starting autonomous queue drain; claiming beads autonomously. |
51	| `review` | Read-only review. Grades an existing implementation against AC or specs. | Read code, docs, and evidence; return a structured verdict with per-AC findings. | Writing commits; claiming beads; modifying the tracker. |
52	
53	### Precedence
54	
55	`bead_execution` supersedes `queue_steward` when DDx invokes a worker
56	explicitly (via `ddx work`, `ddx try <id>`, or an internal execute-bead
57	worker invocation).
58	`direct_user_implementation` supersedes `queue_steward` when the user
59	explicitly asks for code or doc edits unrelated to the queue. `review`
60	supersedes `queue_steward` when the user explicitly requests a graded review
61	pass.
62	
63	Tracker instructions, merge-policy, and safety rules in AGENTS.md are
64	load-bearing in every mode and are never overridden by mode selection.
65	
66	### Lifecycle Migration Startup Gate
67	
68	`ddx work`, `ddx try`, server-managed workers, GraphQL worker starts, REST/MCP
69	worker starts, and queue status/readiness commands must run the TD-027 §18 lifecycle
70	startup preflight before claiming beads or deriving worker eligibility. When the
71	active queue still contains unmigrated legacy lifecycle labels or
72	pseudo-statuses, these surfaces fail closed with a configuration/startup error.
73	They do not call Fizeau, do not classify the failure as an agent attempt, and do
74	not add cooldown or retry metadata.
75	
76	Allowed bypass commands are limited to help/version output, `ddx doctor` or
77	other read-only diagnostics that do not mutate lifecycle, and the lifecycle
78	migration command:
79	
80	```bash
81	ddx bead migrate --lifecycle --dry-run
82	ddx bead migrate --lifecycle --apply
83	```
84	
85	The error must print counts of legacy labels and pseudo-statuses, a small sample
86	of affected bead IDs, and the exact migration command. The migration is one-way:
87	old `needs_human` and `triage:needs-investigation` lanes are not runtime
88	compatibility modes. If the migration is wrong, the recovery path is git
89	rollback of the tracker commit.
90	
91	### Worker-capacity suggestion rules
92	
93	An agent in `queue_steward` mode may suggest running `ddx work` or point
94	the operator to worker-capacity settings when:
95	
96	- The queue contains execution-ready beads that have not been attempted.
97	- The queue is blocked and operator action is required.
98	- The operator asks "why isn't anything running?" or a similar capacity question.
99	
100	The agent must not autonomously start `ddx work` from `queue_steward` mode.
101	Suggesting the command or explaining the conditions is the correct action.
102	
103	## Problem Statement
104	
105	**Current situation:** DDx has accreted two independent run-storage shapes
106	— `.ddx/exec-runs/` for generic execution evidence and
107	`.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/` for tracked bead-attempt bundles. Loop behavior,
108	worktree lifecycle, and bead resolution are spread across legacy command
109	surfaces without a clean layering story. There is no single place to introspect "what runs
110	happened, at what layer, with what evidence."
111	
112	**Pain points:**
113	- Two on-disk record shapes for fundamentally similar artifacts (a single
114	  agent invocation, with logs, structured result, and provenance)
115	- No first-class concept of the task execution layers (`run` / `try` /
116	  `work`), so consumers must intuit which surface to read
117	- Stop conditions for queue drain are implicit and not observable
118	- Generated-artifact provenance has no clean hook into the run record
119	- Open-ended catalog pressure: every new "run flavor" (benchmark, replay,
120	  comparison) tempts a new on-disk shape or a new top-level verb
121	
122	**Desired outcome:** A single on-disk run substrate with explicit layer
123	metadata, three top-level CLI verbs that map 1:1 onto the layers, and a
124	narrow read-only HTTP/MCP write surface limited to artifact regeneration.
125	
126	## Task Execution Layers
127	
128	### Layer 1 — `ddx run` (invocation atom)
129	
130	A layer-1 run is one AI invocation. Inputs are a prompt, requested
131	`MinPower` and optional `MaxPower`, optional operator passthrough constraints,
132	and non-routing execution config; outputs are the structured response (text or
133	bytes), any side-effects the service performed via tools, and run metadata
134	(tokens, model, actual power, duration, exit status, session pointer).
135	
136	Layer 1 calls the upstream Fizeau execution contract directly. DDx does not
137	reimplement routing or the invocation loop; it wraps one `Execute` call with
138	provenance capture.
139	
140	`ddx artifact regenerate <id>` is layer 1 when the generator returns
141	bytes/text and DDx writes the file; it is layer 2 when the generator
142	edits the repo directly (bead-less worktree).
143	
144	### Layer 2 — `ddx try <bead>` (bead attempt)
145	
146	A layer-2 run is one bead attempt in an isolated workspace. It owns:
147	
148	- Workspace creation from a base revision and workspace finalization
149	  (merge or preserve)
150	- Workspace cleanup after finalization, failed setup, failed publish, and
151	  ordinary interruption
152	- Bead → prompt resolution (description, acceptance, governing artifacts)
153	- Side-effect bundling (commits, evidence, no-changes rationale)
154	- One or more layer-1 invocations recorded inside the attempt record
155	
156	A layer-2 record references its child layer-1 records by run id.
157	
158	Layer 2 owns bead-attempt success classification. DDx determines success from
159	the artifacts it owns: commit presence, merge/preserve result, no-changes
160	rationale, post-run checks, review verdicts, and cooldown policy. The agent's
161	exit status and actual model/power are inputs to that decision, not the whole
162	decision.
163	
164	Layer 2 records raw attempt evidence. Layer 3 applies the outcome → state
165	mapping in [`TD-031 §2`](../../02-design/technical-designs/TD-031-bead-state-machine.md)
166	when that evidence requires durable bead action such as close-as-already-satisfied,
167	human triage, blocked, retry cooldown, or stale no_changes metadata cleanup.
168	
169	### Quality hooks
170	
171	ADR-023 defines two lifecycle quality hooks owned by the layer-2 and layer-3
172	execution loop. They are hooks inside `ExecuteBeadLoopRuntime`, not new run
173	layers and not new bead-schema fields.
174	
175	`BeadReadinessHook` is the product concept: it runs after the worker has
176	selected a dependency-ready candidate but before DDx claims the bead or
177	creates the implementation worktree. The canonical decision being made is bead
178	readiness assessment, the pre-claim check for tractability and actionability;
179	lint/rubric scoring is the diagnostic pass inside readiness, and post-attempt
180	triage is a separate after-evidence action.
181	The implementation may still call the compatibility entrypoint
182	`PreClaimIntakeHook` and record `MODE: intake` for legacy compatibility, but
183	those names are aliases only. Within readiness, the nested bead-lifecycle
184	workflow skill performs lint/rubric scoring to support the decision, and the
185	score is diagnostic evidence rather than a separate queue action. Post-attempt
186	triage is separate and runs only after evidence exists. The hook has enough
187	context to evaluate title, description, acceptance criteria, labels, parent,
188	deps, bead type, spec-id, prior attempt history, and whether the bead is
189	atomic enough to execute. The hook invokes the nested bead-lifecycle workflow
190	skill under the `ddx` skill tree and records readiness evidence in the
191	layer-3 run record.
192	
193	`ddx work` wires this hook by default in both CLI and server-managed worker
194	paths. Decomposition decisions request Fizeau's `smart` model-ref and run with a
195	strong `MinPower` floor, defaulting to the smart/top-power class floor when no
196	project-specific splitter override is configured. DDx does not choose the
197	concrete model; it passes the raised power floor to Fizeau and preserves any
198	operator-supplied harness/provider/model passthrough constraints. If those
199	constraints cannot satisfy the strong floor, DDx records readiness as
200	unavailable (`readiness_error` / `intake_error`) instead of treating the bead as
201	ambiguous or in need of human review; the worker then continues through the
202	normal implementation route with the same operator passthrough constraints.
203	
204	The readiness assessment result is one of:
205	
206	- `actionable_atomic` — claim and execute normally.
207	- `actionable_but_rewritten` — DDx applied a validated replacement rewrite or
208	  metadata update that makes the bead more execution-ready before claim. Safe
209	  rewrites may replace noisy, stale, duplicated, or brittle description text
210	  with a fit-for-purpose bead body instead of appending more prompt burden. They may
211	  also format the description into the authoring template, add discovered
212	  file:line evidence, add an obvious test command, or wire deterministic
213	  labels/parent/deps. The mutation is recorded through `ddx bead` paths before
214	  claim, with before/after hashes and enough evidence to audit the replacement.
215	- `too_large_decomposed` — DDx created child, sibling, or replacement beads,
216	  mapped every parent AC to generated ACs or an explicit operator-required /
217	  `non_scope` marker, left the oversized bead `status=open` with dependency,
218	  execution-eligibility, or supersession metadata that lets the queue advance,
219	  and did not execute the oversized bead directly.
220	- `ambiguous_requires_operator` — the bead/spec is unclear, contradictory,
221	  unverifiable, or missing acceptance criteria that DDx cannot safely invent.
222	  DDx moves the bead to `status=proposed` and does not claim it.
223	- `readiness_error` / `intake_error` — readiness infrastructure failed. In
224	  migration/WARN mode this fails open with evidence; in reliable factory/BLOCK
225	  mode it may skip the candidate for the current pass but must not park it
226	  behind a cooldown unless a retryable time-based class is recorded.
227	
228	Safe rewrites are validated against durable context, not against a vague
229	"same spirit" comparison to the prior bead text. The prior bead can itself be
230	the problem: it may contain stale line numbers, duplicated history, chat-shaped
231	noise, or contradictory wording. A safe replacement must preserve explicit
232	commitments that remain valid, especially acceptance criteria, non-scope, named
233	files/tests, dependencies, and governing artifact references; it must either
234	carry forward durable root-cause evidence or replace it with current file:line
235	evidence from the repository or governing artifact. The original text is kept in
236	readiness evidence, not copied into the replacement body. Rewrites must optimize
237	prompt fitness rather than raw length: expanding a one-line underspecified bead
238	is safe when the added durable context is required for execution, and compressing
239	a noisy bead is safe when explicit commitments remain preserved.
240	
241	Readiness must reject the rewrite when preservation cannot be proven from
242	durable anchors. Rejected rewrite does not by itself authorize operator
243	attention: DDx next tries safe decomposition or replacement work when that can
244	preserve explicit scope, and moves the bead to `status=proposed` only when the
245	remaining ambiguity, missing governing artifact, or scope conflict requires
246	operator judgment. It must not invent product behavior, choose between
247	conflicting requirements, change scope, delete unresolved constraints, or guess
248	a missing governing artifact.
249	
250	`PostAttemptTriageHook` runs after the attempt has produced its local evidence:
251	agent result, commit/no-commit state, no-changes rationale if any, post-run
252	checks, adversarial review verdict, merge/preserve result, and the readiness
253	report.
254	It uses the same bead-lifecycle skill to classify whether the outcome is a
255	normal attempt result, a quality-policy failure, a missing-evidence failure, or
256	an infrastructure failure. This is distinct from bead readiness assessment and
257	from the lint/rubric scoring used inside readiness. The hook must never rewrite
258	the attempt result or erase artifacts; it only adds triage evidence and feeds
259	retry/stop classification.
260	
261	If post-attempt triage finds that an implementation attempt stopped because the
262	bead was too large or the worker could not legally decompose inside its
263	worktree/depth context, the layer-3 worker must invoke the same orchestrator
264	decomposition path used by `BeadReadinessHook`. This is machine-actionable
265	work: DDx files child, sibling, or replacement beads, records the AC map, and
266	leaves the oversized bead open with dependency, execution-eligibility, or
267	supersession metadata that lets the queue advance. Only lossy or ambiguous
268	splits require operator attention.
269	The operator is not required merely because the implementer could not split
270	from inside the attempted execution.
271	
272	`ExecuteBeadReport` gains `OutcomeReason` beside the existing `Disrupted`
273	signal. `Disrupted` remains the mechanical indicator that normal completion was
274	interrupted. `OutcomeReason` is the stable classification string that explains
275	why the attempt ended, such as `lint_warn`, `lint_blocked`,
276	`lint_hook_error_fail_open`, `readiness_ambiguous`, `readiness_decomposed`,
277	`triage_missing_evidence`, `review_fixable_gap`, `review_spec_gap`,
278	`review_too_large`, `post_run_check_failed`, or `success`. Layer-3 `ddx work`
279	records aggregate these reasons when evaluating retry eligibility, no-progress,
280	blocked, and deferred stop conditions.
281	
282	The hook evidence lives in the attempt evidence bundle and is surfaced through
283	the unified run substrate. The originating bead may receive an evidence event
284	only for explicit operator override (`--force --reason`) or other existing
285	audit events; lint scores are not copied into `.ddx/beads.jsonl`.
286	
287	### Layer 3 — `ddx work` (queue drain)
288	
289	A layer-3 run is one drain of the bead queue. It iterates `ddx try`
290	across ready beads until a stop condition is met. It owns:
291	
292	- Queue iteration order
293	- Pre-claim readiness, safe bead improvement, decomposition, claim acquisition,
294	  claim release, and shutdown/interruption cleanup for claimed beads, using the
295	  TD-027 §12 claim-state contract
296	- Durable bead action after each layer-2 attempt, using TD-031 §2 outcome,
297	  no_changes, cooldown, and stale-metadata rules
298	- No-progress / stop-condition evaluation
299	- A loop-level record that references its child layer-2 records by
300	  attempt id and reports terminal disposition (drained, blocked,

300	  attempt id and reports terminal disposition (drained, blocked,
301	  deferred, no-progress)
302	
303	Content-aware supervisory decisions (e.g., "comparison failed → enqueue
304	reconciliation beads") are not layer-3 concerns. Those are skill or
305	plugin compositions on top of the task execution layers.
306	
307	Layer 3 treats raw attempt evidence separately from durable bead action. When a
308	worker stops, receives SIGTERM/SIGINT, loses its child agent process, or honors
309	operator cancel before a terminal bead mutation, it follows TD-027 §12.2: preserve
310	evidence, record the interruption/disruption, release the claim, and leave the
311	bead re-claimable unless an explicit retryable cooldown or blocker was recorded.
312	Shutdown is not itself a reason to park a bead on `work-retry-after`.
313	
314	#### Idle-Path Diagnosis and Auto-Remediation
315	
316	When the picker returns no executable bead, Layer 3 does not exit. It diagnoses
317	each non-ready bead in the breakdown (`Epics`, `EpicClosureCandidates`,
318	`DepWaiting`, `OperatorAttention`) with a per-bead reason code, fires the
319	matching auto-remediation in the same loop iteration, then re-scans. The drain
320	exits only when every blocker class has been resolved or has been routed to an
321	operator surface with an exhausted-auto-remediation marker.
322	
323	**Reason taxonomy** (closed enum; new codes require this spec to be updated):
324	
325	- `superseded_pending_close` — bead has `superseded-by:<Y>`, Y is closed.
326	  Drives a one-hop cascade close inside `Close(Y)`.
327	- `closure_candidate_misclassified` — epic with `openChildCount == 0 &&
328	  totalChildCount > 0` mistakenly in the epic-container bucket. Drives a
329	  classifier-correction reroute into the existing closure-evaluation path.
330	- `dead_intermediate_all_children_closed` — `execution-eligible == false`,
331	  all children closed. Drives the same closure path.
332	- `dead_intermediate_open_children_pending` — `execution-eligible == false`
333	  with open children that have their own auto-remediation. Skip; recursion
334	  via the children resolves the parent.
335	- `epic_of_epic` — only open children are themselves epics. Skip; the inner
336	  epic's auto-remediation resolves the chain.
337	- `dep_blocked_by:<id>(status=<s>)` — bead has open deps. No direct action;
338	  the cascade of supersession/closure remediations is expected to unblock
339	  most. Remaining real blocks surface in `ddx work focus` Section A with the
340	  blocker ID and status cited.
341	- `genuinely_needs_decomposition` — `IssueType == epic`,
342	  `totalChildCount == 0`, not closure-pending, no `manual-hold` /
343	  `no-auto-decompose` / `container` operator override, valid decomposition
344	  source (PROBLEM/PROPOSED FIX/AC sections per the bead-authoring template,
345	  plus a `spec:*` or `area:*` label). Drives one dispatch of the pre-claim
346	  decomposer (`runPreClaimDecomposer`) per loop iteration.
347	- `parent_child_state_conflict` — parent/child state mismatch the classifier
348	  does not expect. Surface for operator.
349	- `claimed_in_progress` — bead currently claimed by an active worker. No
350	  action; honored by claim semantics.
351	- `provider_route_unavailable` — last dispatch failed because the selected
352	  provider route is unavailable. In broad `ddx work` drains this remains
353	  retryable: record the failed route, keep the bead open, and let the next
354	  attempt choose a different live route. Targeted runs still surface the
355	  provider hint.
356	- `gated_by_budget_or_cooldown` — auto-remediation matched but
357	  `--max-recovery-cost` exhausted, cooldown active, or `--no-*` override set.
358	  Surface with the gate name.
359	- `malformed_parent_or_dep_ref` — parent/dep ID does not resolve. Surface
360	  for operator (tracker integrity issue).
361	- `dependency_cycle` — cycle detected in dep DAG. Surface for operator.
362	- `closed_or_missing_parent` — parent resolves to closed or non-existent
363	  bead. Surface for operator.
364	- `stale_graph_index` — child/dep index disagrees with raw JSONL scan.
365	  Trigger index rebuild; re-diagnose next loop.
366	- `auto_remediation_exhausted` — attempts cap hit or cooldown active for the
367	  same reason. Surface for operator.
368	- `no_diagnosis` — none of the above matched. Log + surface as bug.
369	
370	**Safety contract (applies to every auto-remediation):**
371	
372	- Per-bead attempt cap of 3 per reason code, tracked via event count.
373	  Beyond the cap the diagnosis becomes `auto_remediation_exhausted` and the
374	  bead surfaces in `ddx work focus` Section A.
375	- Cost budget: `--max-recovery-cost` (default $2). Auto-decompose counts
376	  against it; cheap pure-Go remediations (RC-1 reroute, RC-2 cascade, RC-3
377	  cleanup) do not.
378	- Cooldown: failed auto-decompose calls `SetExecutionCooldown` for 15m to
379	  prevent the same epic from being repicked next scan.
380	- Cycle guard: per-loop-iteration visited set for any recursive operation
381	  (RC-2 cascade, RC-3 walk-up, RC-4 epic-of-epic recursion).
382	- Operator overrides: per-handler kill switches `--no-auto-supersede-close`,
383	  `--no-auto-epic-decompose`, `--no-auto-closure-reclassify`; per-project
384	  defaults under config key `work.autoRemediations`.
385	- Audit: every auto-action emits an event with the reason code, the bead
386	  ID, actor `auto-remediator`, and (for cascade ops) the trigger ID.
387	- Re-scan policy: if any auto-remediation succeeded in the pass, the loop
388	  re-runs `nextCandidate()` without sleeping. Otherwise it falls through to
389	  the existing `sleepOrWake` idle path.
390	- Throughput: at most one auto-decompose dispatch per loop iteration to bound
391	  cost; cheap remediations batch in a single pass.
392	- Read-only paths (`ddx work plan --explain`, `ddx work focus`) never mutate;
393	  only the live `ddx work` loop fires auto-actions.
394	
395	**Operator surface (`ddx work focus`)** is two sections:
396	
397	- **Section A — Operator-required:** beads with diagnoses that have no
398	  automated remediation or have exhausted attempts.
399	- **Section B — Auto-remediating next loop:** one-line count of beads with an
400	  auto-action queued, with `--verbose` opening the per-bead detail. Silent
401	  hiding of auto-remediable beads is forbidden — operators must always be able
402	  to see what the loop is about to do.
403	
404	The epic-specific behavior under this contract is documented in FEAT-004
405	"Queue Semantics For Epics" and inherits all safety guards above.
406	
407	## Agent Power and Retry
408	
409	DDx owns retry policy between bead attempts. If an attempt produces classified
410	evidence that more capable reasoning could plausibly change the result, DDx may
411	retry the bead with a higher `MinPower`.
412	
413	Fizeau owns routing within the requested power bounds. A retry request says
414	"use at least this much power" by raising `MinPower`; an explicit `MaxPower`, if
415	supplied, remains a hard upper bound. Operator-supplied `--harness`,
416	`--provider`, and `--model` values may be sent to Fizeau as passthrough
417	constraints, but DDx does not interpret them. Each layer-1 record stores:
418	
419	- requested `MinPower` and optional `MaxPower`
420	- requested passthrough constraints, if any
421	- actual model and actual power reported by Fizeau
422	- run outcome and DDx attempt outcome
423	
424	DDx may query Fizeau's available model/power catalog to choose escalation
425	targets such as "retry using only top-power models." DDx uses the returned power
426	numbers only to compute `MinPower` thresholds; Fizeau still chooses the
427	concrete model.
428	
429	Passthrough constraints stay sticky across retries. DDx may increase `MinPower`
430	between attempts, but it must not alter or drop operator-supplied
431	harness/provider/model passthrough values to make escalation work. If the
432	combination is invalid or constraining, the agent owns that typed error or
433	actual route. DDx stops with `blocked_by_passthrough_constraint` or
434	`agent_power_unsatisfied`, records the evidence, and reports operator action
435	required; it does not remove pins, widen pins, call any route-selection helper,
436	or retry in a loop.
437	
438	### Retry eligibility
439	
440	Power retry is eligible only when DDx-owned evidence shows that a stronger model
441	could plausibly help after a valid attempt started. Eligible classes include:
442	
443	- `capability_insufficient` — the agent attempted the bead, but reasoning
444	  quality or implementation quality was insufficient.
445	- `post_run_check_failed` — tests or gates failed after a valid checkout and
446	  attempted change.
447	- `review_fixable_gap` — adversarial review found implementation or test gaps
448	  plausibly addressable by another automated cycle.
449	- `no_changes_after_attempt` — the agent had a valid checkout and task context
450	  but produced no usable change, subject to the no-progress budget.
451	
452	Power retry is not eligible for deterministic setup or operator-action
453	failures:
454	
455	- dirty worktree, merge/land conflict, missing checkout, invalid bead metadata,
456	  unresolved dependencies, config parse errors, missing harness binaries,
457	  authentication failures, command-not-found/toolchain setup failures
458	- readiness ambiguity, missing acceptance criteria, spec contradictions,
459	  decomposition overflow, review errors, reviewer context overflow, claim races,
460	  routing preflight rejection, quota/transport disruption, and any other class
461	  where the implementer did not receive valid task context and an opportunity to
462	  act
463	- `blocked_by_passthrough_constraint` / `agent_power_unsatisfied`
464	- any status where retry would require DDx to inspect, remove, rewrite, or
465	  substitute `--harness`, `--provider`, or `--model`
466	
467	Every retry/stop decision records the classification and evidence used. The
468	policy may inspect DDx attempt outcomes and the agent's typed status; it must
469	not branch on concrete provider/model identity.
470	
471	ADR-024 is the decision record for this policy. FEAT-010 owns when `ddx try` or
472	`ddx work` schedules a retry, how no-progress and stop conditions are recorded,
473	how review-error retry scopes are counted, and how budget caps stop a drain.
474	FEAT-006 owns only the agent request envelope used by those decisions.
475	
476	### Execution Cycle Traceability
477	
478	A bead execution is an append-only chain of implementation/review cycles. Layer
479	3 owns the chain; layer 2 owns the evidence for each cycle. A repair attempt
480	must create a new cycle record rather than overwrite the rejected one.
481	
482	Each cycle records:
483	
484	- `cycle_index`
485	- `attempt_id`
486	- `base_rev`
487	- `result_rev` when a stable candidate exists
488	- implementer layer-1 run ids, requested power bounds, actual route/power, and
489	  cost/usage facts
490	- verification command output and exit status
491	- `review_group_id`, reviewer layer-1 run ids, reviewer requested/actual
492	  route facts, aggregate verdict, per-AC evidence summary, and reviewer cost
493	- prior review linkage (`repair_context_from_review_group`) when this cycle is
494	  a repair attempt
495	- final cycle decision: close, retry, decompose, block, manual-required, or
496	  budget stop
497	
498	When a review requires another implementation cycle, the next implementation
499	prompt includes the prior review group's findings as required repair context.
500	The prior cycle remains immutable so operators can answer what changed, why the
501	first result was rejected, and why the final result passed.
502	
503	### Candidate-Cycle Pipeline
504	
505	A **candidate** is the stable commit produced by one implementation pass and
506	pinned as the subject of the review gate before land. A candidate exists when
507	the implementation produces at least one commit and post-run checks have been
508	attempted.
509	
510	A **candidate ref** is a project-root git ref
511	(`refs/ddx/iterations/<attempt-id>/<cycle-index>`) that pins the candidate
512	commit so reviewers can inspect a stable revision even after worktree cleanup.
513	
514	A **cycle** is one implementation pass plus its associated review gate: one
515	implementation pass that produces a candidate, the read-only tool reviewer gate
516	run against that candidate in the still-live worktree, and the resulting
517	disposition (land, repair, or stop).
518	
519	A **repair cycle** is a cycle triggered when the prior cycle's review verdict is
520	`review_fixable_gap`. The repair implementation runs in the same still-live
521	attempt worktree and is **append-only** — no `git reset`, `git commit --amend`,
522	`git squash`, or `git rebase` against the prior candidate is permitted. The
523	repair prompt includes the prior review group's findings as required context.
524	
525	A **read-only tool reviewer** is a reviewer agent that runs in the still-live
526	attempt worktree with access to read-only filesystem tools (file reads,
527	searches) but no write tools. The bounded evidence bundle assembled per FEAT-022
528	is the canonical review input; same-worktree read-only tool access is
529	supplemental and does not override the bounded bundle. The reviewer cannot
530	create commits or modify worktree state.
531	
532	**Terminal dispositions** for a candidate-cycle attempt:
533	- `landed` — candidate was approved and merged to the base branch.
534	- `preserved` — candidate is approved but land was deferred; worktree retained.
535	- `parked` — automatic repair, review retry, reframe, and decomposition paths
536	  exhausted without an executable next step; bead moved to `status=proposed`.
537	- `conflicted` — approved candidate failed to land due to merge conflict; bead
538	  returned to `open` for re-attempt with a fresh `base_rev`.
539	- `budget-stopped` — drain-level cost or no-progress budget tripped; workspace
540	  preserved with evidence.
541	
542	**Candidate-cycle sequence** (in the still-live attempt workspace, before land):
543	
544	1. Create isolated workspace from `base_rev`.
545	2. Run implementation pass; collect `base_rev`, `result_rev`, run ids,
546	   verification output, and cost facts.
547	3. Pin candidate ref; emit `candidate-pinned`.
548	4. Run candidate checks (post-run tests, lint, gates). Failure emits
549	   `candidate-checks-failed`; plausibly capability-sensitive failures are
550	   eligible for a repair cycle when `repair_max_cycles` allows.
551	5. Dispatch read-only tool reviewers in the still-live workspace, **before land**.
552	   Record `review_group_id`, reviewer run ids, per-AC verdict, and cost.
553	6. On unanimous `APPROVE`: land, emit `final-result-landed`, close bead, clean up.
554	7. On `REQUEST_CHANGES` / `BLOCK` classified `review_fixable_gap` within
555	   `repair_max_cycles`: emit `repair-cycle-started`, run append-only repair in
556	   same workspace, return to step 3.
557	8. On `repair_max_cycles` exhausted: emit `repair-cycle-exhausted`, preserve
558	   workspace, and return the bead to the TD-031 auto-recovery path. Move to
559	   `status=proposed` only after automatic reframe/decompose/replacement cannot
560	   produce executable follow-up work or the finding requires operator judgment.
561	9. On approved candidate that fails to land (merge conflict): emit
562	   `approved-land-conflict`, release claim, return bead to `open`.
563	
564	**Budget axes** (each is distinct from cost, rate-limit/provider, and
565	no-progress budgets):
566	
567	- **`repair_max_cycles`** — maximum repair cycles per attempt. Exhaustion fires
568	  `repair-cycle-exhausted` and enters the TD-031 auto-recovery path; it moves
569	  the bead to `status=proposed` only after automatic recovery fails or requires
570	  operator judgment. It does not consume the no-progress counter.
571	- **`review_max_retries_per_candidate`** — maximum reviewer retry attempts for a
572	  single candidate ref when reviewer invocations fail. Exhaustion returns to
573	  automatic recovery unless the error class proves operator action is required;
574	  only operator-required exhaustion fires `review-manual-required`. A new
575	  candidate ref (next repair cycle or fresh `ddx try`) resets this counter
576	  independently; it is strictly per-candidate.
577	
578	These axes do not interact with the drain-level cost cap (FEAT-014), the
579	rate-limit retry budget internal to a single attempt, or the no-progress counter
580	tracking consecutive attempts with no commit.
581	
582	**Transitional single-slot allowance.** Until the two-slot quorum reviewer bead
583	lands, a single reviewer slot satisfies the review gate. The removal condition
584	is the landing of the bead that implements two-slot quorum aggregation.
585	
586	### Escalation, fallback, retry, and review decision tree
587	
588	The layer-3 drain evaluates each ready bead through this mechanical sequence:
589	
590	1. **Eligibility and readiness.** Pick a dependency-ready candidate. Run the
591	   readiness gate. Validated replacement rewrites happen before claim and should
592	   leave a clearer implementation prompt, expanding underspecified beads or
593	   compressing noisy/stale beads as the durable context requires; original text
594	   is preserved in readiness evidence for audit. Too-large work is decomposed
595	   before an implementation attempt, using sibling or replacement decomposition
596	   when child depth is exhausted. Ambiguous or underspecified work moves to
597	   `status=proposed` only when the gate reaches a hard operator-required
598	   condition after safe rewrite/decomposition options are exhausted; otherwise
599	   WARN-ONLY findings proceed on the open forward-progress lane. Readiness
600	   infrastructure failure records evidence and follows the
601	   configured fail-open/factory-mode policy; it never creates an unexplained
602	   cooldown.
603	2. **Claim.** Claim only an `actionable_atomic` or safely rewritten bead. Claim
604	   races skip the bead for the current pass without cooldown.
605	3. **Primary implementation cycle.** Run one layer-2 implementation attempt.
606	   A candidate result records `base_rev`, `result_rev`, implementation run ids,
607	   route/power facts, verification output, and cost. Already-satisfied
608	   no-changes may close only after the required verification evidence exists;
609	   otherwise no-changes outcomes follow TD-031 §6.1 NoChangesContract.
610	4. **Adversarial pre-close review.** Review is enabled by default. For every
611	   close-eligible `result_rev`, layer 2 pins a candidate ref and dispatches two
612	   read-only tool reviewer runs **in the still-live attempt worktree, before
613	   land**, each carrying `role=reviewer`, a stronger `MinPower` floor than the
614	   implementer, and correlation metadata (`review_group_id`, `result_rev`,
615	   reviewer slot, and implementer route facts). The bounded evidence bundle is
616	   the canonical review input; same-worktree read-only tool access is
617	   supplemental. Close is permitted only when the aggregate review is unanimous
618	   `APPROVE` with per-AC evidence.
619	5. **Review classification.** Any evidenced `REQUEST_CHANGES` or `BLOCK`
620	   prevents close. `review_fixable_gap` schedules a repair cycle on the same
621	   bead when `repair_max_cycles` allows, injecting the review findings as
622	   required repair context and optionally raising `MinPower`; it preserves the
623	   retry path and does not move the bead to `status=proposed`.
624	   `review_spec_gap`, `review_missing_acceptance`, `review_too_large`, and
625	   non-mechanical unsafe or out-of-scope findings enter the TD-031
626	   decomposition-first path: safe reframe, child decomposition, and
627	   sibling/replacement decomposition are attempted when they can preserve
628	   explicit scope. DDx moves the bead to `status=proposed` only when those paths
629	   would be lossy or require operator judgment, and it does not ask another
630	   implementer to guess. Malformed, empty, context-overflow, and transport
631	   reviewer failures emit `review-error` scoped to `result_rev` and reviewer
632	   slot; after `review_max_retries_per_candidate` they return to automatic
633	   recovery unless the error class proves operator action is required. Only
634	   operator-required review errors emit `review-manual-required`, clear the
635	   active claim, move the bead to `status=proposed`, and prevent close. The
636	   `review_fixable_gap` path stays on the automatic retry track instead of
637	   converting into operator review.
638	6. **Infrastructure fallback.** Transport, quota, rate-limit, command setup,
639	   context cancellation, routing preflight rejection, and worker disruption are
640	   not model-capability failures. They emit structured evidence and either stay
641	   immediately retryable or use a bounded retry-after when the same class is
642	   time-based. HTTP 429 retry happens inside one attempt with `rate-limit-retry`
643	   events.
644	7. **Capability retry.** Failed checks, `review_fixable_gap`,
645	   capability-insufficient attempts, and eligible no-changes-after-attempt may
646	   schedule a higher-power retry by raising the next request's `MinPower`.
647	   Passthrough constraints remain unchanged. If the requested power cannot be
648	   satisfied under those constraints, DDx records a terminal operator-action
649	   classification and stops retrying that bead.
650	8. **Cost stop.** Implementation and review attempts both contribute reported
651	   billable cost to the drain budget according to FEAT-014 cost-class metadata.
652	   When the cap trips, the drain records a budget stop and stops claiming new
653	   work; the cap is not reported as model failure.
654	
655	## Substrate Unification
656	
657	### One record shape
658	
659	All task execution layers persist to a single on-disk shape under
660	`.ddx/runs/<run-id>/`. The record has the following common fields and a
661	discriminating `layer` field; layer-specific extensions live in named
662	subobjects rather than separate file trees.
663	
664	Common fields:
665	- `run_id` — stable, sortable identifier
666	- `layer` — `1` | `2` | `3`
667	- `parent_run_id` — pointer to the enclosing layer's run, if any
668	- `started_at` / `finished_at`
669	- `terminal_status` — `success` | `failure` | `timeout` | `error` |
670	  `no_progress` (layer 3 only) | `preserved` (layer 2 only)
671	- `actor`, `host`, `git_revision`, `ddx_version`
672	- `produces_artifact` — optional artifact id when this run regenerates
673	  an artifact
674	- `attachments[]` — references to large bodies (logs, structured payloads,
675	  bundles) stored alongside the record rather than inline
676	
677	Layer extensions:
678	- `layer1` — prompt reference, agent config, model id, token usage,
679	  upstream session id, structured response pointer, normalized
680	  `ToolCallEntry` stream captured at drain time
681	- `layer2` — bead id, base revision, workspace path, finalization mode
682	  (`merge` | `preserve`), child layer-1 run ids, evidence-bundle pointer
683	- `layer3` — queue snapshot pointer, stop-condition evaluation log,
684	  child layer-2 attempt ids, drain disposition
685	
686	Records are append-only. A new run never rewrites a previous one.
687	
688	### On-disk migration
689	
690	The two pre-existing trees collapse into the unified substrate:
691	
692	- `.ddx/exec-runs/<run-id>/` → `.ddx/runs/<run-id>/` with `layer: 1`
693	- `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/` → `.ddx/runs/<run-id>/` with `layer: 2`
694	  and the existing prompt / manifest / result / checks / provenance
695	  surfaced as layer-2 evidence-bundle attachments
696	
697	Layer-3 records are new (no pre-existing tree).
698	
699	Migration is one-way and read-compatible:
700	- Writers always target `.ddx/runs/`
701	- Readers tolerate legacy paths during a documented migration window
702	  and surface them through the unified read API as if they were layer-1
703	  or layer-2 records, then a one-shot rewrite normalizes them on disk
704	- After the migration window closes, legacy paths are removed; readers
705	  no longer fall back
706	
707	The migration window, the rewrite tool, and the cutoff signal are
708	specified in the implementation epic, not here.
709	
710	The lifecycle-state migration is separate from this run-substrate migration.
711	TD-027 §18 owns that one-way queue migration contract. Unlike legacy run-record
712	readers, normal task execution does not read through old lifecycle labels after
713	the startup gate is enabled.
714	
715	## `ddx work` Stop Conditions
716	
717	`ddx work` drains the queue until **any** of the following stop
718	conditions is met. The terminal disposition of the layer-3 record names
719	which condition fired.
720	
721	1. **`drained`** — queue is empty (no ready beads remain).
722	2. **`blocked`** — every remaining ready bead has produced a terminal
723	   non-success outcome on its most recent attempt and re-attempt is not
724	   permitted under the current policy.
725	3. **`deferred`** — a configured wall-clock or attempt-count budget is
726	   exhausted. The remaining queue is intact and a subsequent `ddx work`
727	   resumes from where this one stopped.
728	4. **`no_progress`** — `N` consecutive valid implementation attempts produced
729	   no commit and no merged side-effect on the base branch (default `N = 3`;
730	   configurable). A valid implementation attempt is one where DDx created a
731	   checkout, supplied task context to the implementer, and the implementer had
732	   an opportunity to act. Intake blocks, decomposition, claim races, routing
733	   preflight rejection, quota/transport/auth/tool setup failures, review errors,
734	   and operator-action classes do not increment this counter.
735	5. **`signal`** — a SIGINT/SIGTERM was received. On the first signal, DDx
736	   prints `Cancel received, shutting down gracefully`, cancels the active
737	   command context, stops claiming new beads, and gives the in-flight
738	   `ddx try` (if any) one cooperative cleanup path to preserve evidence,
739	   release its claim, and leave the bead re-claimable. A second signal may
740	   hard-abort the process.
741	6. **`resource_exhausted`** — DDx cannot safely create or publish execution
742	   state because a required execution root is out of bytes, out of inodes, not
743	   writable, or cannot register/remove git worktrees. Resource exhaustion is
744	   host infrastructure failure, not bead failure and not model no-progress.
745	   `ddx work` stops claiming new beads after recording the failed `ddx try`
746	   setup evidence and a cleanup summary. It must not continue to the next
747	   ready bead in the same drain.
748	
749	Stop-condition evaluation normally runs **between** `ddx try` invocations.
750	`signal` is the exception: the first signal cancels an in-flight attempt
751	cooperatively rather than waiting for the model to finish. The cancelled
752	attempt is mechanically disrupted, not a model no-progress failure, and ordinary
753	interrupt shutdown MUST NOT park the bead behind `work-retry-after`.
754	
755	### Execution Resource Cleanup
756	
757	DDx-created execution resources must have an owner, a liveness signal, a
758	retention policy, and a cleanup path. Missing ownership, missing liveness, or
759	unbounded accumulation is a correctness bug in the execution substrate.
760	
761	Layer 2 owns inline cleanup for one `ddx try` attempt. Before claim or
762	workspace creation it validates the required execution roots:
763	
764	- the temporary execution root, resolved in this order: `DDX_EXEC_WT_DIR`,
765	  `executions.temp_worktree_root`, then the per-user cache root
766	  (`$XDG_CACHE_HOME/ddx/exec-wt` on Linux, or the platform cache equivalent)
767	- the durable evidence root, currently `.ddx/executions` during migration and
768	  `.ddx/runs` for new substrate records
769	- workspace backend setup/teardown for the project repository
770	
771	The validation checks writability, free bytes, and free inodes where the
772	platform exposes inode counts. If validation fails, `ddx try` runs one
773	immediate DDx-scoped cleanup pass, re-checks, and then either proceeds or
774	returns `resource_exhausted` without claiming the bead.
775	
776	The attempt backend is selected by `--attempt-backend` or
777	`executions.attempt_backend`. `worktree` is the default linked git worktree
778	backend. `local-clone` creates a full local clone under the same execution root
779	and imports result commits back into the project repo before land. `docker-clone`
780	uses that clone as the Docker working tree; it requires
781	`executions.docker.image` and can set Docker memory, CPU, pids, tmpfs, and
782	network limits under `executions.docker`. `in-tree` (opt-in) runs the attempt
783	directly in the project checkout; it requires clean working tree before claim,
784	enforces exclusive single-worker locking, and is designed for data-heavy
785	validation tasks that need access to project-local state (untracked datasets,
786	running services, checked-out fixtures). On failure with dirty tree, the bead is
787	unclaimed and a rescue message is left for manual recovery.
788	
789	`ddx work` and server-managed workers use the same cleanup manager before the
790	first claim and before later claims whenever any checked temp or evidence root
791	falls below the soft cleanup trigger of **512 MiB free bytes** or **8192 free
792	inodes**. If cleanup does not restore all roots above the hard stop floor of
793	**64 MiB free bytes** and **1024 free inodes**, the loop stops visibly with
794	`resource_exhausted` and claims no more beads.
795	
796	After an attempt starts, Layer 2 removes the isolated workspace when the result
797	has been merged, explicitly preserved, classified as no-changes/no-evidence, or
798	interrupted through the cooperative shutdown path. Failed setup must remove any
799	partial unregistered directory it created. A workspace may remain only when DDx
800	records an explicit preserve decision with evidence pointing at the retained
801	path or ref.
802	
803	DDx-owned cleanup scope includes execution workspaces, helper scratch roots
804	created beside the configured execution root, legacy `$TMPDIR/ddx-exec-wt`
805	resources, DDx-created test and e2e scratch roots, generated test binaries, and
806	run-state or liveness files. The cleanup manager may delete only DDx-owned
807	paths. Recognized DDx-owned scratch prefixes are: `ddx-test-`, `ddx-e2e-`,
808	`ddx-test-bin-`, `ddx-test-binary-`, `ddx-lifecycle-`,
809	`ddx-agent-support-keepalive`, `ddx-config-anchor-`, `ddx-exec-keepalive`,
810	`ddx-metric-keepalive`, `ddx-metaprompt-keepalive`,
811	`ddx-persona-keepalive`, `ddx-vale-`, `ddx-gate-wt-`,
812	`ddx-land-finalize-`, `ddx-land-wt-`, `ddx-push-recover-`, and
813	`ddx-conflict-recover-`. Any temp directory that contains a `cleanup.json`
814	metadata file with matching project ownership is also DDx-owned regardless of
815	its name.
816	
817	Deletion is permitted only when all of the following hold:
818	
819	- **Metadata-backed paths**: the `cleanup.json` liveness record is expired or
820	  the owning attempt has reached a terminal state.
821	- **Metadata-less recognized-prefix paths**: the directory's mtime is at least
822	  **6 hours** old and no live PID or active session is attached.
823	
824	The manager must preserve published evidence, active workspaces, and anything
825	outside DDx-owned roots.
826	
827	Layer 3 owns loop cleanup. `ddx work` runs cleanup:
828	
829	- once at startup, before the first queue claim
830	- before the next claim after any setup/finalization failure
831	- periodically while a long-lived poll worker remains active
832	- during graceful signal shutdown before exit
833	
834	Long-lived DDx processes also start a background cleanup worker. The background
835	worker runs occasionally with jitter and a project-level cleanup lock so multiple
836	workers do not all prune at once. It is conservative and DDx-scoped: it may
837	remove stale unregistered directories under DDx temp roots, registered DDx
838	worktrees or metadata-backed workspaces whose attempt is terminal or whose
839	liveness marker is stale, stale
840	heartbeat/liveness files for dead PIDs, and partial setup directories that were
841	never published as complete evidence. It must not remove preserved workspaces,
842	`refs/ddx/iterations/...`, complete `.ddx/runs/<id>` or
843	`.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>` evidence, active workspaces with live
844	PID/session liveness, or non-DDx directories.
845	
846	Cleanup is observable but not noisy. Routine passes are trace/debug or worker
847	events. Passes that reclaim significant bytes or inodes emit an operator-visible
848	summary such as `cleanup: removed 37 stale ddx worktrees, freed 14210 inodes`.
849	Resource exhaustion after cleanup is a hard visible stop message and a layer-3
850	`resource_exhausted` disposition.
851	
852	Cleanup reporting includes scratch roots removed, bytes and inodes reclaimed,
853	preserved paths, and blocked warnings so operators can see why cleanup stopped
854	short.
855	
856	### `ddx work` Run Modes
857	
858	`ddx work` supports three named run modes that govern when the worker exits.
859	These are the public operator semantics; there are no other mode names.
860	
861	| Mode | CLI flag | Semantics |
862	|---|---|---|
863	| `once` | `--once` | Process at most one execution-ready bead, then exit immediately. |
864	| `drain` | (default, no flag) | Process all currently execution-ready beads until a terminal stop condition fires, then exit. The worker does not sleep or wait for new work to arrive. |
865	| `watch` | `--watch` | Process ready work, then remain idle and rescan for newly arriving execution-ready work at each idle interval, until `signal`, fatal config error, budget/resource stop, or operator stop fires. |
866	
867	**Idle interval** is the sleep duration between empty-queue scans while in `watch`
868	mode. The public flag is `--idle-interval <duration>` with a default of **30s**
869	in watch mode. There is no idle interval concept in `once` or `drain` modes.
870	
871	**`--poll-interval` is removed from the public CLI and generated docs.** There is
872	no legacy alias. Operators previously relying on `--poll-interval 30s` to
873	stay alive should use `--watch`. Operators previously relying on
874	`--poll-interval=0` to drain-and-exit should use bare `ddx work` (drain mode,
875	the new default).
876	
877	#### Direct CLI defaults
878	
879	Bare `ddx work` with no mode flag uses **drain** mode: the worker processes all
880	currently execution-ready beads and exits when a stop condition fires. Operators
881	who want long-running watch behavior must pass `--watch`.
882	
883	#### Server-managed worker defaults
884	
885	Server-managed workers spawned via `POST /api/workers/work` default to
886	**watch** mode with a **30s idle interval** when the request omits `mode` and
887	`idle_interval`. The request vocabulary uses `mode` + `idle_interval` fields:
888	
889	```json
890	{
891	  "mode": "once" | "drain" | "watch",
892	  "idle_interval": "<duration-string>"
893	}
894	```
895	
896	`poll_interval` is not accepted as a server/API input. Workers previously
897	launched with `poll_interval` must migrate to `mode` + `idle_interval`.
898	
899	The worker record exposes a `substate` field set to `"idle"` while the loop is
900	sleeping between empty scans in watch mode; it is cleared as soon as a candidate
901	is picked. Terminal worker states (`exited`, `failed`, `stopped`, `reaped`)
902	always clear `substate`.
903	
904	The structured `loop.end` event carries an `exit_reason` field (`once_complete`,
905	`drain_complete`, `sigint`, `sigterm`, `fatal_config`, `resource_exhausted`;
906	`providers_exhausted` is reserved for the quota-pause work in ddx-aede917d).
907	
908	Power escalation is evaluated between attempts as part of retry policy. A
909	higher-power retry raises `MinPower` and resets neither the evidence history nor
910	the no-progress counter; it is recorded as the next `ddx try` with its own
911	requested bounds and actual power metadata.
912	
913	The evaluation log is persisted on the layer-3 record so a human or tool can
914	audit which condition fired and on which iteration.
915	
916	### `ddx work` Stdout Contract
917	
918	This section defines the human-facing stdout produced by `ddx work`. Every item
919	here is a stable operator-visible surface; the structured event log (JSONL) is a
920	separate machine-readable channel.
921	
922	#### Terminal summary
923	
924	When `ddx work` exits for any reason, it prints a terminal summary block. The
925	summary states:
926	
927	- **Attempts:** total bead attempts made in this drain.
928	- **Closed:** count of beads closed during this drain.
929	- **Changed / already-satisfied:** breakdown of closed beads into those that
930	  produced a commit (`changed`) and those closed as already-satisfied with no
931	  commit (`already-satisfied`).
932	- **Failures:** count of attempts that ended in a non-success terminal outcome.
933	- **Stop reason:** the named stop condition that fired (`drained`, `blocked`,
934	  `deferred`, `no_progress`, `signal`, or `resource_exhausted`).
935	- **Remaining queue state:** a summary of open beads broken into buckets (see
936	  below).
937	
938	Example terminal summary:
939	
940	```
941	ddx work — drain complete
942	  attempts:          7
943	  closed:            5  (changed: 4, already-satisfied: 1)
944	  failures:          2
945	  stop reason:       drained
946	
947	remaining queue:
948	  execution-ready:   0
949	  waiting-on-deps:   3
950	  proposed:          1
951	  cooldown/deferred: 1
952	  not-eligible:      0
953	  superseded:        0
954	  epic/closure:      0
955	```
956	
957	#### Remaining queue-state buckets
958	
959	The remaining queue-state summary distinguishes the following buckets where DDx
960	can compute them:
961	
962	| Bucket | Definition |
963	|---|---|
964	| `execution-ready` | Beads whose dependencies are satisfied and that have no outstanding block |
965	| `waiting-on-deps` | `status=open` beads with unsatisfied dependencies; derived waiting, not `status=blocked` |
966	| `blocked` | Beads with `status=blocked` because an external recheckable blocker is recorded |
967	| `proposed` | Beads awaiting operator decision before autonomous execution |
968	| `cooldown/deferred` | Beads on retry-after cooldown or explicitly deferred |
969	| `not-eligible` | Beads in a non-runnable state (closed, cancelled, superseded-pending-removal) |
970	| `superseded` | Beads explicitly superseded by later work |
971	| `epic/closure-candidate` | Parent/epic beads that may be closable when all children complete |
972	
973	#### Operator-review dependency pressure
974	
975	When open beads are transitively waiting behind `status=proposed` operator
976	review blockers, the terminal summary and each watch-mode idle poll include a
977	dependency-pressure section. The wording is:
978	
979	```
980	30 beads waiting behind 3 proposed blockers:
981	  ddx-a1b2c3d4 "resolve auth ambiguity" (12 downstream)
982	  ddx-e5f6a7b8 "clarify rate-limit spec" (11 downstream)
983	  ddx-c9d0e1f2 "investigate flaky test" (7 downstream)
984	```
985	
986	**Total waiting count** is the number of unique open downstream beads that are
987	transitively waiting behind one or more proposed operator-review blockers — the
988	deduplicated union across all such blockers. **Per-blocker
989	downstream count** is the count of unique open beads that are transitively
990	waiting through that specific blocker. Per-blocker counts may sum higher than
991	the total when a bead is waiting behind multiple operator-review blockers.
992	
993	#### Watch mode idle stdout
994	
995	While in watch mode with an empty queue, every idle poll prints a compact
996	queue-state line:
997	
998	```
999	idle: no execution-ready beads; sleeping 30s [execution-ready: 0, waiting-on-deps: 3, proposed: 1]
1000	```
1001	
1002	The idle line always includes: the literal `idle:` label, a reason phrase, the
1003	sleep duration, and a compact inline queue-state summary with at minimum the
1004	`execution-ready`, `waiting-on-deps`, and `proposed` counts.
1005	
1006	The full blocker list (blocker IDs, titles, and per-blocker downstream counts)
1007	may be suppressed on repeated idle polls when the queue state is unchanged from
1008	the prior poll. The counts and sleep duration remain visible on every poll.
1009	
1010	#### Next-ready-bead transition stdout
1011	
1012	When watch mode wakes up from an idle sleep and claims new work, stdout separates
1013	the new bead from the prior idle block with a transition line printed before the
1014	bead execution block starts:
1015	
1016	```
1017	taking next ready bead from queue: ddx-a1b2c3d4 — "implement login rate limiting"
1018	```
1019	
1020	The format is `taking next ready bead from queue: <id> — <title>`.
1021	
1022	## Quality Hooks
1023	
1024	ADR-023 (`../../02-design/adr/ADR-023-bead-lifecycle-quality-policy.md`)
1025	adds two quality hooks to the layer-2/layer-3 lifecycle. The hooks are
1026	implemented in `ExecuteBeadLoopRuntime` at the same boundary that already
1027	owns bead selection, attempt finalization, and retry classification.
1028	
1029	`BeadReadinessHook` runs after a bead has been selected and verified as
1030	dependency-eligible, but before DDx claims it or creates the implementation
1031	worktree. It performs the canonical bead readiness assessment, the pre-claim
1032	decision about tractability and actionability; lint/rubric scoring is the
1033	diagnostic pass inside readiness, and post-attempt triage is a separate
1034	after-evidence action. The legacy compatibility name `PreClaimIntakeHook` and
1035	`MODE: intake` may still appear in migration code or notes, but they are not
1036	the product concept.
1037	
1038	### Forward-Progress Readiness Policy
1039	
1040	**Open is the execution lane.** `status=open` is the default runnable state for
1041	beads that are not yet closed, superseded, or awaiting external blockers.
1042	Readiness findings do not move beads from `open` to `proposed` unless a hard
1043	operator-required condition exists after safe rewrite and decomposition options
1044	are exhausted.
1045	
1046	**Proposed is the operator-decision escape.** `status=proposed` is an explicit
1047	operator-review gate. An operator moves a bead from `proposed` to `open` when
1048	they accept it as actionable despite readiness warnings or ambiguity.
1049	
1050	**Operator-promoted beads are durable.** When an operator moves a bead from
1051	`status=proposed` back to `status=open` (operator promotion), readiness must not
1052	move that same bead back to `proposed` for the same rule or finding unless:
1053	- A prompt-relevant field (description, acceptance, labels, parent, deps) has
1054	  changed materially, **or**
1055	- The operator explicitly requests re-triage via `--force`
1056	
1057	This non-regression clause prevents readiness from recreating a proposed→open→proposed
1058	loop after an operator has already made an explicit acceptance decision.
1059	
1060	Readiness assessment reports must be diagnostic and distinct from hard parking
1061	decisions. In WARN-ONLY mode (the default per ADR-023), readiness warnings
1062	proceed by default, and only a hard operator-required condition may park the
1063	bead in `status=proposed`. BLOCK mode (opt-in) may stop dispatch on a valid
1064	low readiness score, but it still honors the operator-promotion non-regression
1065	clause. The staged rollout and factory mode details are in ADR-023 "Staged
1066	Rollout And Factory Mode" and "Operator-Promotion Non-Regression Clause".
1067	
1068	The hook receives the bead record, current execution policy, hook mode
1069	(`WARN-ONLY` or `BLOCK`), and the layer-3 evidence directory. It invokes the
1070	bead-lifecycle workflow skill from FEAT-011 and writes a bead readiness,
1071	scope, and decomposition report. In WARN-ONLY mode, the report is diagnostic
1072	and the open forward-progress lane continues unless the hook can safely
1073	improve or decompose the bead or reaches a hard operator-required condition.
1074	In BLOCK/factory mode, a valid low readiness score, unsafe ambiguity, or
1075	too-large classification stops implementation before claim.
1076	
1077	`PostAttemptTriageHook` runs after the attempt has produced its owned
1078	evidence: commits or no-changes rationale, command results, review verdicts,
1079	merge/preserve result, and any structural validation result. It invokes the
1080	same bead-lifecycle workflow skill against the attempt bundle and returns an
1081	attempt classification such as prompt-quality issue, task-quality issue,
1082	infrastructure failure, deterministic setup failure, or operator action
1083	required. This classification feeds retry reporting and operator UX; it does
1084	not replace the existing success/no-changes/failed outcome taxonomy.
1085	
1086	Both hooks are fail-open for infrastructure failures. If the skill package is
1087	missing, the hook process crashes, evidence cannot be written, or the model
1088	invocation fails, DDx records the hook error and continues with the underlying
1089	attempt flow in WARN-ONLY mode. In BLOCK/factory mode, hook infrastructure
1090	failure may skip the current candidate for the pass with observable evidence,
1091	but it must not park the bead behind an unexplained cooldown. Only a
1092	successfully computed readiness/lint result can block dispatch as an authoring
1093	or scope failure. `--force --reason <text>` records an event with the actor,
1094	reason, hook mode, and overridden criteria before proceeding.
1095	
1096	Long-running entrypoints perform a lightweight DDx runtime preflight before
1097	starting work. This is not a full `ddx doctor` run: it avoids network checks,
1098	package-manager checks, route probing, stale-document scans, and other broad
1099	diagnostics. The preflight is limited to conditions that can predictably degrade
1100	the command about to run:
1101	
1102	- project root resolution;
1103	- `.ddx/config.yaml` parseability;
1104	- `.ddx/beads.jsonl` parseability for bead-execution commands;
1105	- TD-027 §18 lifecycle migration status for queue-derived and worker-start
1106	  commands;
1107	- the root `ddx` project skill under `.agents/skills/ddx/SKILL.md` or
1108	  `.claude/skills/ddx/SKILL.md`;
1109	- the nested `bead-lifecycle` skill when readiness/lint/triage hooks are wired;
1110	- legacy project-local skill symlinks from the pre-FEAT-015 install model.
1111	
1112	Passing preflight checks are silent. Failed checks are observable and
1113	actionable. `ddx work` and `ddx try` fail on missing required project/tracker
1114	state. Unmigrated lifecycle queues fail closed before worker startup with the
1115	TD-027 §18 migration counts, affected bead sample, `ddx bead migrate --lifecycle --dry-run`,
1116	`ddx bead migrate --lifecycle --apply`, and git rollback guidance. Optional
1117	lifecycle-hook degradation in WARN-ONLY mode still warns once per process and
1118	continues after the lifecycle migration gate is clean. `ddx server`
1119	fails on missing required server project state, but missing lifecycle skills or
1120	legacy skill layout are surfaced as degraded startup diagnostics and health
1121	metadata rather than blocking the HTTP server. When the lifecycle skill is
1122	missing, both startup preflight and hook-time errors must include the project
1123	root, the exact checked paths, and remediation:
1124	
1125	```text
1126	Run:
1127	  ddx update --force
1128	  ddx doctor
1129	```
1130	
1131	`ExecuteBeadReport` gains an `OutcomeReason` field beside the existing
1132	`Disrupted` flag. `Disrupted` remains the coarse boolean that the attempt did
1133	not complete normally. `OutcomeReason` is the machine-readable reason selected
1134	by lifecycle classification, for example:
1135	
1136	- `bead_lint_warn`
1137	- `bead_lint_blocked`
1138	- `readiness_actionable_atomic`
1139	- `readiness_rewritten`
1140	- `readiness_decomposed`
1141	- `readiness_proposed`
1142	- `forced_with_reason`
1143	- `triage_prompt_quality`
1144	- `triage_task_quality`
1145	- `triage_infrastructure_failure`
1146	- `triage_operator_action`
1147	- `hook_unavailable_fail_open`
1148	
1149	Layer-3 `ddx work` records include the hook summaries for each child layer-2
1150	attempt in the stop-condition evaluation log. A blocked lint result counts as a
1151	terminal non-success outcome for that bead under the current policy, so the
1152	existing `blocked`, `deferred`, and `no_progress` loop rules can reason about
1153	it without a fourth run layer or a separate run type.
1154	
1155	## No Run-Type Catalog
1156	
1157	DDx will not introduce additional run kinds beyond `run`, `try`, and `work`.
1158	Comparison, replay, benchmark, adversarial review, effort estimate,
1159	bead breakdown, and similar workflows are **skill compositions** over
1160	the task execution layers — they emit ordinary layer-1, layer-2, or layer-3
1161	records, optionally tagged with a free-form `skill` label, and do not get
1162	bespoke storage shapes, bespoke verbs, or bespoke spec sections.
1163	
1164	This is a load-bearing non-goal: every future "we should add a fourth
1165	layer for X" proposal is rejected by reference to this section. If a
1166	new workflow cannot be expressed as a composition over `run` / `try` /
1167	`work`, the gap is in the task execution contract, not in the catalog.
1168	
1169	## Requirements
1170	
1171	### Functional
1172	
1173	1. **One on-disk substrate** — all run records, regardless of layer,
1174	   persist under `.ddx/runs/<run-id>/` with the common record shape and
1175	   a discriminating `layer` field.
1176	2. **Layer metadata** — every run record carries `layer`,
1177	   `parent_run_id`, and the layer-appropriate extension subobject.
1178	3. **Layer-1 invocation** — `ddx run` produces exactly one layer-1
1179	   record per invocation; consumes upstream Fizeau execution contract
1180	   per CONTRACT-003.
1181	4. **Layer-2 attempt** — `ddx try <bead>` produces exactly one layer-2
1182	   record, references its child layer-1 records, and finalizes the
1183	   worktree as `merge` or `preserve`.
1184	5. **Layer-3 drain** — `ddx work` produces exactly one layer-3 record
1185	   per invocation, references its child layer-2 attempt ids, and reports
1186	   one of the terminal dispositions (`drained`, `blocked`, `deferred`,
1187	   `no_progress`, `signal`, `resource_exhausted`).
1188	6. **Stop conditions** — `ddx work` evaluates the stop conditions
1189	   between attempts and persists the evaluation log on the layer-3
1190	   record.
1191	7. **No-progress detection** — the `no_progress` condition is mechanical
1192	   (no commit, no merged side-effect) and configurable via
1193	   `.ddx/config.yaml` (default `N = 3`).
1194	8. **Append-only history** — a new run never rewrites a previous record
1195	   at any layer.
1196	9. **Cross-layer query** — `ddx runs` lists records across all three
1197	   layers with layer filtering; `ddx tries` and `ddx work workers`
1198	   are layer-specific projections over the same substrate.
1199	10. **Run detail inspection** — for any run id, callers can retrieve the
1200	    common fields, the layer extension, attachments, and child run ids.
1201	11. **Generated-artifact provenance** — runs that regenerate an artifact
1202	    set `produces_artifact: <id>` on the run record; FEAT-007 reads this
1203	    to populate `generated_by` graph edges.
1204	12. **Migration from legacy paths** — readers tolerate
1205	    `.ddx/exec-runs/` and `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/` during a
1206	    documented migration window and surface them as layer-1 / layer-2
1207	    records; writers always target `.ddx/runs/`.
1208	13. **Configuration** — substrate root, no-progress threshold, deferred
1209	    budget, and retention settings are configurable in
1210	    `.ddx/config.yaml`.
1211	14. **Execution cleanup** — `ddx try` and `ddx work` remove stale DDx-owned
1212	    execution worktrees, DDx-created test/e2e scratch roots, generated test
1213	    binaries, and liveness files through inline, loop, and background cleanup
1214	    paths without deleting preserved attempts or published evidence.
1215	15. **Resource preflight** — `ddx try` validates writable execution roots and
1216	    free bytes/inodes before claim; failed validation may trigger one cleanup
1217	    retry, and `ddx work` / server-managed workers run cleanup before claim
1218	    whenever temp free space drops below a soft high-water threshold. If the
1219	    temp roots remain below the hard floor after cleanup, the loop returns
1220	    `resource_exhausted` without claiming.
1221	16. **Network-free drain boundary** — `Land()` performs only the
1222	    worktree-merge into local main, under a brief local lock. Origin-sync
1223	    (`git fetch` + merging `origin/main` into local main) and origin-push
1224	    (`git push`) are separate operations, operator-driven or
1225	    background-deferred, and are never invoked from `Land()` or from the
1226	    drain loop. See FEAT-023 for the designated origin-sync command.
1227	17. **Automatic recovery from persistent failure** — when a bead's within-cycle
1228	    escalation ladder is exhausted on `N` consecutive drain cycles (default
1229	    `N = 2`, configurable in `.ddx/config.yaml` as
1230	    `escalation.auto_recovery_threshold`), `ddx work` attempts
1231	    (a) reframe via a strong-power reframer agent, then (b) decomposition via
1232	    a strong-power decomposer agent, before parking the bead at
1233	    `status=proposed`. Each recovery step follows ADR-024 P4 and P3 (strong
1234	    `MinPower` floor, passthrough constraints forwarded unchanged). Outcomes
1235	    and status transitions are defined in TD-031 §2 (`reframe_applied`,
1236	    `decompose_applied`, `auto_recovery_failed`) and the sequence in
1237	    SD-025 Layer 3.5.
1238	
1239	Expected implementation tests include `TestExecutionCleanup_RemovesStaleDDXScratchDirs`,
1240	`TestWorkResourcePreflight_RunsCleanupBelowSoftFloor`, and
1241	`TestWorkResourcePreflight_StopsBelowHardFloorAfterCleanup`.
1242	
1243	### Non-Functional
1244	
1245	- **Per-bead cost budget:** every bead's escalation attempts (implementation,
1246	  review, reframer, and decomposer invocations) are bounded by a configurable
1247	  per-bead cost cap (`escalation.per_bead_budget_usd` in `.ddx/config.yaml`).
1248	  When the cap is exceeded, DDx records `per_bead_budget_exhausted` (TD-031 §2),
1249	  releases the claim without cooldown, and leaves the bead `status=open` and
1250	  re-claimable. Budget exhaustion is a recheckable signal, not a terminal state;
1251	  an operator may raise the cap or the bead may be retried in a later drain.
1252	  See ADR-024 Per-Bead Budget.
1253	- **Determinism:** the persisted record reflects the exact prompt,
1254	  response, logs, and structured result of one invocation.
1255	- **Durability:** record writes are atomic or serialized so concurrent
1256	  writers cannot leave partial records.
1257	- **Observability:** stored records are human-readable enough for
1258	  debugging and machine-parseable enough for automation.
1259	- **Portability:** records remain repo-local and file-backed; no hosted
1260	  service or database is required.
1261	- **Low overhead:** layer-1 record bookkeeping adds minimal overhead
1262	  beyond the underlying agent invocation.
1263	- **Payload resilience:** large prompt, response, log, and bundle
1264	  bodies are stored as attachments and never require rewriting a shared
1265	  history file to persist one run safely.
1266	- **Network isolation:** single-developer/single-node drain does not block
1267	  on remote I/O; origin-sync and origin-push are decoupled and may run in
1268	  parallel or in the background without holding the drain or land lock.
1269

1310	| (GraphQL) | `Mutation.runRequeue` | Re-queue the originating bead of an existing run record (manual operator re-queue from the Runs UI); requires `idempotencyKey`; emits a canonical `run_requeue` audit event on the bead (schema below) | **write — narrow** |
1311	
1312	The HTTP/MCP read surface is total over the unified run substrate.
1313	
1314	The write surfaces added by this feature are limited to
1315	`artifactRegenerate` and `runRequeue`. All other run invocation
1316	(layer-1 ad-hoc, layer-2 bead attempts, layer-3 queue drain) remains

1318	they are not implicit in this read-coverage expansion.
